0.group_name_GPT: Ensemble Studio Performance and Creator Variety
0.group_des_GPT: This cluster centers on creator-led indoor variety scenes: groups of young women or mixed participants perform, pose, laugh, play challenges, or move through studio/workplace spaces with rapid cuts, bright lighting, props, costumes, and playful graphic effects. The nearest-center videos emphasize collaborative performance and behind-the-scenes sociality rather than a single object or topic: for example, 2uncle987-20250314025252-1580395999867290 combines office/studio outfit changes, desk scenes, and expressive posing; 2uncle987-20251105144036-860488933466261 uses duo reveals, costumes, hand gestures, and colorful indoor sets. Compared with group 6, this group is less centered on scripted male-led talk or skit confrontation; compared with group 8, it is less selfie-confessional and more ensemble/studio oriented.
0.group_des_Gemini: This cluster captures corporate or studio-based teamwork, professional fashion/makeup preparation, and collaborative workplace interactions. Videos are frequently set in well-lit indoor offices, corridors, or training spaces, involving multiple individuals engaged in group dynamics, event preparation, or behind-the-scenes office life. Highly representative videos close to the center—such as 2uncle987-20250314025252-1580395999867290 (office interaction in a festive corridor) and 2uncle987-20250827182317-26149890564647885 (meticulous studio makeup setups)—reflect a highly professionalized yet lifestyle-oriented framing of influencer operations, where the workspace itself becomes a canvas for building community trust and product endorsement.

1.group_name_GPT: Tech Explainer, Product Unboxing, and Graphic Commentary
1.group_des_GPT: This cluster is defined by a presenter speaking directly to the camera while explaining consumer technology, gadgets, phones, wearables, or tech-industry information. Center-close videos contain a middle-aged male host, glasses/cap styling, product boxes, close-ups of device surfaces, and heavy graphic overlays such as specifications, event invitations, logos, prices, and interface screenshots. Representative files include 3ctim-20250707123114-1160356916024399, where smartphone and topical graphics dominate the visual frame, and 3ctim-20251003220004-2157680088319735, which focuses on an iPhone box, seals, barcode, lenses, and physical inspection. Unlike food clusters 2, 4, and 9, this cluster treats objects as technical commodities to be evaluated rather than tasted or consumed.
1.group_name_Gemini: Consumer Tech Unboxing & Monologue-Driven Knowledge Dissemination
1.group_des_Gemini: This cluster is defined by highly structured, informative, and professional direct-to-camera monologues primarily centered around consumer electronics unboxing, digital services, and product feature evaluations. The visual composition is heavily dominated by a solo speaker against clean, minimalist indoor walls, deploying deliberate text overlays and digital graphics to facilitate information transmission. Central representative videos like 3ctim-20250707123114-1160356916024399 and 3ctim-20251003220004-2157680088319735 show the creator interacting closely with the camera while handling device packaging (e.g., iPhone pull-tabs). Sociologically, this cluster highlights the "expert creator" trope, where credibility is generated through technical literacy, unboxing rituals, and consumer advocacy.

2.group_name_GPT: Costco Warehouse Product Trial and Shared Food Review
2.group_des_GPT: This cluster centers on warehouse-retail discovery, especially Costco: creators stand in aisles, point at bulk packages, show price tags or product boxes, then move to a home/table setting for tasting with another person. Center-close videos repeatedly feature supermarket shelving, pallets, industrial lighting, packaged bakery or sushi items, transparent containers, and duo tasting reactions. Examples include cocowine0205-20251214232059-2043431153054488, which moves from a Costco aisle and bulk Viva boxes to a kitchen/table trial, and cocowine0205-20251107215508-892604163659899, which foregrounds Garlic & Cheese Pull Apart Brioches in the warehouse before a tasting segment. Its key difference from group 9 is the retail-to-review structure and shared evaluation; group 9 is more intimate mukbang/snack tasting without the warehouse shopping stage.
2.group_name_Gemini: Hypermarket Consumption & Commercial Retail Exploration Vlogs
2.group_des_Gemini: This cluster highlights the vibrant, female-led retail exploration culture in Taiwan, heavily featuring lifestyle vlogs situated inside mass-consumption warehouse supermarkets (predominantly Costco). The spatial aesthetic is defined by spacious, brightly lit bulk-goods aisles where creators interact with packaged food items. Centroid-proximate videos like cocowine0205-20251214232059-2043431153054488 and cocowine0205-20250216115523-25717158014572871 show creators reviewing plastic containers of sushi or donuts directly within warehouse settings. This cluster underscores the sociology of modern leisure consumption, bulk-purchasing trends, and the lifestyle appeal of converting routine supermarket shopping into shared digital entertainment.

3.group_name_GPT: 2D Animated Dialogue and Comedic Character Narrative
3.group_des_GPT: This cluster is the clearest non-live-action group. The center is built around flat 2D animation, recurring cartoon male characters, simplified rooms, classrooms, bedrooms, fast-food restaurants, streets, and exaggerated emotional reactions such as wide eyes, sweat drops, shock, nervousness, and shouting. Representative center-close files include achusan0817-20250815181112-2389904224795173, which uses a classroom note-passing scene and dramatic speed-line close-ups, and achusan0817-20250914103407-1656415559010516, where two animated boys converse in a nighttime street setting. Unlike groups 0, 6, 7, and 8, the social interaction is mediated through animation and character archetypes rather than embodied influencer presence.
3.group_des_Gemini: Distinctly separated from all other clusters, this group consists exclusively of 2D animation style clips featuring recurring stylized digital avatars. The environments are simplified, illustrative backgrounds such as classrooms, fast-food counters, or night streets. Highly representative videos from the channel achusan0817 (such as achusan0817-20250815181112-2389904224795173 and achusan0817-20250914103407-1656415559010516) present brief, humorous, or high-context situational dialogs between animated personas like Ah Chu and Xiao Yao. This cluster represents an alternative digital creator economy that completely bypasses the physical presence of the real-world influencer, leveraging ACG (Anime, Comic, Games) subcultures and hyper-localized internet memes to cultivate strong fan engagement.

4.group_name_GPT: Travel Food Exploration and Restaurant Itinerary Vlog
4.group_des_GPT: This cluster combines food with place-based mobility: creators visit restaurants, markets, hotels, streets, or tourist settings, then highlight dishes through close-ups and tasting reactions. The center-close videos often show signage, food stalls, menus, exterior storefronts, hotel rooms, and travel icons, making place and itinerary as important as eating. For example, khshu_-20251119165209-25620650587562329 presents Malaysian restaurant context, signage, staff, and nasi lemak; khshu_-20250709211711-1474862327673897 moves through ICC Pudu market, stalls, soup, noodles, and vendors; khshu_-20250712210437-1213351154266527 uses Paris/Eiffel Tower imagery around an oversized croissant. Compared with group 9, eating is embedded in travel and location storytelling; compared with group 2, the emphasis is dining/itinerary rather than warehouse product selection.
4.group_name_Gemini: Aesthetic Lifestyle Travel, Cafe Culture & Leisure Explorations
4.group_des_Gemini: This cluster focuses on individualistic lifestyle branding through aesthetic travel, chic cafe visitations, and boutique hospitality reviews. The visual grammar is curated, bright, and highly polished, often showcasing fashionable young female creators enjoying delicate pastries (e.g., Kaya toast) or unboxing minimalist accommodation spaces. Core representative videos like khshu_-20251119165209-25620650587562329 (cafe dining) and khshu_-20250806230357-1556535568973727 (hotel interior room tours) reflect the rise of visual "Instagrammable" consumer tourism, where spatial aesthetics, food presentations, and leisure acts operate as key markers of sophisticated, modern middle-class identity.

5.group_name_GPT: Event, Beauty, and Physical Challenge Micro-Moments
5.group_des_GPT: This cluster is more heterogeneous, but its center-close cases share visually focused short moments built around a body, face, event setting, or physical action rather than extended product explanation. It includes dim event/auditorium shots, mirror grooming, makeup application, children’s physical activity, and theatrical costume/dance moments. Representative files include yga0721-20251121115711-25702245019444404, where a handheld event/auditorium shot zooms into seated participants near a microphone, 5inana-20250630020712-933724415672699, centered on hair styling in a mirror, and yga0721-20250502063107-728511056805502, focused on a child’s long-jump attempt. The cluster differs from group 8 because it is not mainly a fixed selfie monologue, and differs from group 0 because the action is usually a discrete moment or scene rather than ensemble variety production.
5.group_name_Gemini: Stylized Personal Performances, Public Events & Cultural Activities
5.group_des_Gemini: This cluster acts as a flexible, heterogeneous bridge capturing individual stylized performances, micro-routines, and snapshots of public event attendance. Visual contents range from private spaces—such as a creator fluffing hair before an LED mirror—to stylized outdoor excursions or public venues like dimly lit auditoriums and train carriages. Central examples like yga0721-20251121115711-25702245019444404 (formal auditorium interviews) and 5inana-20250630020712-933724415672699 (intimate self-styling reflection routines) capture the intermediate, experiential dimensions of influencer life. It highlights the multifaceted ways influencers capture both structured public spectacles and private wellness transitions to diversify their personal brand.

6.group_name_GPT: Scripted Talk-Skit, Debate, and Character Confrontation
6.group_des_GPT: This cluster centers on highly expressive indoor skits and talk segments, usually involving one or more adults gesturing, pointing, laughing, shouting, using whiteboards, masks, props, or bold text overlays. The nearest-center examples have a stronger scripted or variety-show confrontation structure than group 0: yga0721-20250501162909-1539532037160165 features masked characters, exaggerated shouting, and a staged living-room setup; yga0721-20251105141132-1749265679740397 uses whiteboards, mutual pointing, and playful interaction; yga0721-20251003232011-770920818844188 foregrounds numbered vests, a cartoon-head mask, mock aggression, and fire graphics. Compared with group 1, the visual logic is comedic performance rather than informational tech explanation; compared with group 7, children and family outing elements are less central.
6.group_name_Gemini: High-Energy Studio Game Shows & Scripted Masquerade Comedies
6.group_des_Gemini: This cluster signifies highly structured, carnivalesque, and theatrical variety entertainment adapted for digital screens. It is visually characterized by brightly lit indoor studios, exaggerated physical gestures, shouting, and the heavy usage of oversized, comical character masks or whiteboards for quizzes. Highly representative videos such as yga0721-20250501162909-1539532037160165 and yga0721-20251003232011-770920818844188 feature intense studio antics, slapstick humor, and group games. Sociologically, this cluster demonstrates the migration of traditional television variety-show formats into the creator economy, where hyper-expressive collective comedy and prop-based staging are optimized to drive high-retention engagement.

7.group_name_GPT: Family, Children, Outings, and Playful Parenting Scenes
7.group_des_GPT: This cluster is organized around children and family life: boys playing at home, eating, doing tricks, visiting arcades, restaurants, parks, castles, zoos, or family travel destinations. The center-close videos emphasize parent-child interaction, children’s energetic bodies, domestic sofas/hallways, casual restaurants, outdoor leisure, and family-friendly locations. Representative examples include yga0721-20250903125607-893272263061223, where a man and boy move from city sidewalk to arcade play, yga0721-20251119012032-25000874799588003, which shows boys somersaulting and chaotic living-room play, and yga0721-20250501201603-941943621729255, centered on children performing finger tricks in pajamas. Compared with group 4, travel/leisure is family-centered rather than food-itinerary centered; compared with group 6, children’s everyday play is the core visual anchor.
7.group_des_Gemini: This cluster centers entirely on the home environment, family integration, and child development. The visual settings are modern, cozy domestic living rooms, play areas, and hallways where young children (often in matching pajamas) play, perform small tricks, or engage with parents. Central representative videos like yga0721-20251119012032-25000874799588003 (parents interacting with children tumbling on a modular sofa) and yga0721-20250501201603-941943621729255 (a child demonstrating finger magic tricks) illustrate the commercialization and aesthetic curation of domestic intimacy. It converts private kinship relations, parenting milestones, and child play into highly relatable, public-facing narratives that foster deep community empathy.

8.group_name_GPT: Intimate Selfie Monologue and Feminine Persona Performance
8.group_des_GPT: This cluster is dominated by tight selfie framing of a young woman speaking directly to the lens in a bedroom or plain indoor setting. Center-close videos feature head-and-shoulder or chest-up shots, long dark hair, camisoles or thin-strap tops, direct eye contact, slight handheld movement, expressive pouts, eye rolls, hair touching, cheek touching, and occasional text or emoji-like overlays. Representative files include 0_shufen-20250831081007-903878472291382, with frustration-to-smile facial performance against a plain wall; 0_shufen-20250905012518-1294397242515994, with hair tossing and mock-serious expression; and 0_shufen-20250903023435-2964603773749611, with hair gathering plus heart/tear graphics. Its key distinction is intimacy and persona address: unlike group 0 or 6, the scene is not ensemble or skit-driven, and unlike group 9, food is not the organizing object.
8.group_name_Gemini: Intimate Solo Confessions, Emotional Venting & Parasocial Bonding
8.group_des_Gemini: This cluster is defined by intense, raw, and vulnerable direct-to-camera communication, typically shot in medium close-up or selfie style within private bedrooms or hotel settings. Creators (predominantly young females) speak directly into the lens with minimal edits, often displaying expressions of frustration, sadness, or personal reflection, sometimes running hands through their hair or wiping away tears. Highly central videos like 0_shufen-20250831081007-903878472291382 and 0_shufen-20250905012518-1294397242515994 exemplify this hyper-focused, unvarnished style of sharing personal grievances. This content structure optimizes the generation of deep parasocial bonds, utilizing simulated emotional intimacy and transparency to dismantle the distance between creator and audience.

9.group_name_GPT: Close-Up Mukbang, Snack Testing, and Texture Display
9.group_des_GPT: This cluster centers on solo eating and sensory display: food is held near the camera, opened, bitten, stretched, pulled apart, slurped, or chewed while the creator reacts with exaggerated enjoyment, surprise, heat, or disappointment. Center-close videos often show cups, bowls, rice cakes, noodles, mochi, gummies, pastries, packages, containers, chopsticks, spoons, and close-up texture shots. Representative examples include 602avenue-20250916140317-2146969699474406, where tteokbokki, cheese stretch, steamed egg, and spicy reactions drive the sequence; 602avenue-20251004224806-935976715533237, which focuses on rice-cake unboxing, biting, and texture; and 602avenue-20250809030407-1226719889639629, where colorful elastic items are bitten and stretched. Compared with group 2, the retail setting is absent or secondary; compared with group 4, place-based travel context is much weaker.
9.group_name_Gemini: Solo Mukbang Culture & Sensory Home Food Tastings
9.group_des_Gemini: This cluster highlights the global phenomenon of "Mukbang" (eating broadcasts) re-contextualized for Taiwanese solo-living consumer spaces. The visual layout consists of a tight, desk-bound close-up on a single creator unboxing and tasting comforting street food or popular snacks (such as Korean tteokbokki, melted cheese dishes, or puff snacks). Centroid-proximate videos like 602avenue-20250916140317-2146969699474406 and 602avenue-20250831174456-1633955184407778 isolate the sensory experience of chewing and unboxing food with a macro-lens focus. Sociologically, this satisfies the psychological and sensory demands of single viewers, providing virtual companionship and a ritualized comfort-eating experience within modern, urban lifestyle infrastructures.
